title: I did the best I could with what I knew
subtitle: A short affirmation for releasing the loop of replaying past money decisions.
excerpt: A short affirmation for the replaying of old money decisions — the overspent year, the investment that didn't land, the period when the numbers went wrong. Use when the guilt or regret from a past financial choice is loud today. Not when reviewing current finances.
sourceNotes: Adapted from Day 5 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Release shame about past mistakes." The affirmation shape (present-tense, positive construction, repetition) follows public-domain affirmation craft, with roots in the New Thought movement of the late 19th century and modern-form popularisation through writers like Louise Hay (You Can Heal Your Life, 1984).

practice.whenToUse: Use when a past money decision is replaying and the guilt or regret is active today. Not when reviewing current finances or making a decision.

content.text: A short affirmation for the replaying of old money decisions. The overspent year. The investment that didn't land. The period when the numbers went wrong and the consequences followed. When the guilt from those moments is active today, it judges the past with knowledge that didn't exist then. This affirmation returns the memory to its actual context.

Every financial decision made in the past was made with the information, resources, and emotional state available at the time. Judging those decisions from now, with more experience and more distance, is comparing an earlier version against a later one. The affirmation interrupts that comparison.

attrs.quote: I did the best I could with what I knew.

content.text: Three times, slow. Hand on the chest if that helps the words land in the body rather than the head. Say it out loud or in the mind — both work. Let the meaning settle on the third repetition.

Use it as soon as the replaying starts: when a specific memory surfaces, when a number brings back the guilt, when the comparison voice is loudest. Return to this one affirmation each time the pattern appears rather than switching between different ones.
If the shame loop runs too fast for an affirmation to interrupt it, tap first.

Adapted from Day 5 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025), where releasing shame about past financial decisions is the day's focus. The affirmation shape (present-tense, positive construction, repetition over time) follows public-domain affirmation craft, with roots in the New Thought movement and modern-form popularisation through writers like Louise Hay (You Can Heal Your Life, 1984).
